data.title: Micron, Accenture Set To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages closed at record highs on Thursday, a day after the Federal Reserve cut interest rates by 25 basis points. Stocks were propelled by gains in big tech and cybersecurity. Markets were helped by strong premarket action from tech firms Intel (INTC) and Nvidia (NVDA) after the firms announced a blockbuster deal to co-develop PC and data center chips. Also lifting sentiment, the FOMC on Wednesday announced its decision, trimming its policy rate by 25 basis points to 4.00%-4.25%.

After the much-awaited Fed decision, markets will be looking closely at the economic data due to be released next week. These include Chicago Fed National Activity on Monday, S&P Global Manufacturing PMI, and Richmond Manufacturing Index on Tuesday, with reports on MBA Mortgage Applications, New Home Sales, Crude Oil Imports, and Gasoline Inventories to be released on Wednesday. Thursday will see the release of Core PCE Prices, GDP, and Initial Jobless Claims, while the PCE Price Index, Personal Income, Personal Spending, and Michigan Consumer Sentiment will be out on Friday.

Micron (MU), AutoZone (AZO), Cintas (CTAS), Worthington Steel (WS), Costco (COST), Accenture (ACN), CarMax (KMX), BlackBerry (BB), and Inventiva (IVA) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, September 22: Firefly Aerospace (FLY), Genfit (GNFT). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, September 23: Micron, AutoZone, AAR (AIR). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, September 24: Cintas, Worthington Steel, Stitch Fix (SFIX). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, September 25: Costco, Accenture, CarMax, BlackBerry, Inventiva. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, September 26: Deep Yellow (OTCQX:DYLLF), Taylor Devices (TAYD). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Tesla (TSLA) and Wolfspeed (WOLF) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on an increased level of options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Churchill Capital Corp. X (CCCX), Willow Lane Acquisition (WLAC), and Tourmaline Bio (TRML). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include FS KKR Capital (FSK), FactSet (FDS), and Oxford Square (OXSQ).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Broadcom (AVGO), Logitech (LOGI), Humana (HUM), and Ralph Lauren (RL).

IPO Watch: HW Electro (HWEP) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading during the week. The quiet period expires on Tryhard (THH) and Picard Medical (PMI) to free up analysts to post ratings. StubHub (STUB) heads into week two of trading.

Micron Earnings Preview: Micron Technology is poised to release its fourth quarter fiscal 2025 financial results next week. The consensus estimate calls for adjusted earnings per share of $2.86 on revenue of $11.11B. Susquehanna recently increased its price target on the stock as analysts expect the memory maker to produce strong results and outlook on the back of higher-value products. The firm believes Micron's shift towards higher-value products such as high-bandwidth memory will help propel the stock.

Accenture Earnings Preview: Accenture is set to announce its fourth-quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting earnings per share of $2.97 on revenue of $17.34B. Investment firm Stifel recently lowered its price target on the technology consulting firm, citing negative sentiment. The investment firm noted "perceived higher risk areas," such as digital marketing, application services, and government spending due to the cuts made by the Department of Government Efficiency. The headwinds are "legitimate," analyst David Grossman said, but the fears could wind up exceeding reality, he posited.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GameStop: The Tide Has Turned (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
There have been very few people over the last few years who have been as bearish about video game retailer GameStop (NYSE:GME) as I have been. Since I originally rated it a ‘strong sell’ way back in January of 2021, expressing significant doubt about its valuation at the time, the stock has plummeted 69.7%. When stacked up against the 76.3% rise that the S&P 500 enjoyed over the same window of time, this clearly makes the company one of my best calls in recent years. And as recently as June of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a ‘sell’ candidate because of continued weakness and challenges that the business faced. Since then, however, shares have risen by 11.6%. This allowed the company to beat out, if barely, the 10.1% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time.

Looking at the data today, I must say that the picture is improving markedly. I definitely believe that the likelihood of a bankruptcy scenario is incredibly limited. This is especially because of the large net cash position that the company has amassed. Some investors might point out a surge in revenue, profits, and cash flows, as reasons to be optimistic as well. And to some extent, I would agree with this assessment. To be clear, some of the strength that the company exhibited on a year-over-year basis is almost certainly attributable to one-time events. But other aspects of the growth story likely represent a permanent shift in the health of the business. In light of these improving circumstances, I have finally decided to upgrade the company to a very soft ‘hold’ rating. This is the first time since I rated it a ‘strong sell’ in January of 2021 that I have been able to take a neutral stance. And it brings me significant joy considering how near and dear to my heart the business is.

The picture is getting better

For the most recent quarter, financial performance achieved by GameStop was strong. Total revenue for the company came in at $972.2 million. That represents an increase of 21.8% compared to the $798.3 million the company reported the previous year. This surge in revenue came in spite of the fact that software sales contracted from $207.7 million to $152.5 million. I have long believed, though I no longer feel as strongly, that the software side of things was the true hope of the business. And while I don't feel quite that strong anymore, I do believe that this decline in revenue associated with this part of the firm is disconcerting and needs to be evaluated.

The increase in revenue for the company came from its other two categories of product sales. The first of these is the hardware and accessories side of the business, which resulted in $592.1 million in revenue in the most recent quarter. That represents an increase of 31.2% compared to the $451.2 million reported the previous year. This is where investors do need to temper their expectations. Although this rise in revenue was significant, it will likely end up being temporary. I say this because it was almost certainly heavily influenced by the launch of Nintendo’s Switch 2, which was released in June of this year.

According to a third party source, when it comes to the US, overall console sales amounted to 1.63 million units in June of this year, followed by about 456,499 units in July. Globally, unit sales amounted to 5.44 million this June, with that number dropping to just under 1.60 million in July. Naturally, this would be a boon for any company that is dedicated to selling not only the consoles themselves, but also other accessories. But at some point, sales will hit a more normalized pace. I expect, for instance, for hardware and accessories revenue generated by GameStop to be higher in the third quarter of this year compared to the same time last year. But we should notice a meaningful deceleration after Christmas. Of course, sales will probably still remain elevated to some extent for several quarters. This is because, just recently, Nintendo decided to increase its forecast for the total number of global units of the Switch 2 to be sold by March of 2026 from 15 million to 20 million.

Honestly, if this was the only thing that we were seeing, I would still keep my bearish assessment. I don't put a lot of weight on transitory gains. However, that does not appear to be the case. While there might be a short term benefit from these sales, the company is seeing growth in revenue elsewhere also. Namely, I'm talking about a collectibles market. During the quarter, these amounted to $227.6 million in revenue. That represents a massive increase of 63.3% compared to the $139.4 million that the company reported one year ago. This includes a wide array of products, including the Funko Pops! created by toy company Funko (FNKO), figurines, playing cards, and more. In fact, I wrote an article in March of this year discussing how GameStop stands to benefit from a key segment in the collectibles market. Namely, this is the sale, but also the grading of, collecting cards such as Pokémon and Yu-Gi-Oh!.

Based on this data, the collectibles portion of the company now accounts for 23.4% of overall revenue. Unfortunately, we don't know how profitable those sales are. But in all likelihood, with the possible exception of software sales, they are probably the highest category in terms of profit margin. I would venture to say that some of the growth in the collectibles side of the business can be attributed to increased foot traffic at the firm's stores as more consumers came in to purchase consoles and related accessories. But I struggle to imagine it would be such a surge in revenue if that was all.

As revenue for the company increased, profitability improved also. Net income skyrocketed from $14.8 million to $168.6 million. There were two primary drivers behind this. The first of these was a contraction in the company's selling, general, and administrative costs from $270.8 million to $218.8 million. According to management, this was largely because of the firm's decision to reduce its labor force, to cut consulting services that it was receiving, and to reduce marketing spending by $30.3 million, with all of this aimed at cutting costs. Management also said that store related costs and depreciation dropped probably a combined $27.8 million as management accelerated store closures and international divestitures.

The other major category was a rise in interest income, which grew from $39.5 million to $79.6 million. The fact of the matter is that management continues to benefit from a large net cash position and high-interest rates. It is worth noting that some of the gain that the company saw also came from an unrealized gain on digital assets amounting to $28.6 million. During the second quarter of this year, management purchased 4,710 Bitcoin (BTC-USD). And as Bitcoin appreciated, so did the unrealized gain that the company was able to book. Unfortunately, I don't think investors should put very much value in the prospect of Bitcoin appreciating. It is my professional opinion that it's destined to plummet. But a gain is a gain all the same.

All other profitability metrics for the company improved also. Adjusted net profits, as an example, jumped from $5.2 million to $138.3 million. Operating cash flow shot up from $68.6 million to $117.4 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a surge from $16.1 million to $135.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from negative $18 million to positive $75.7 million.

For the first half of this year, financial performance was strong because of the second quarter. Revenue of $1.70 billion beat out the $1.68 billion reported the previous year. As the chart above illustrates, profits and cash flows improved also. But again, this was because of the heavy lifting that the company saw during the second quarter of this year. I will say that, even though I maintain that the rest of this year will probably be positive, there have been headwinds when it comes to the console industry itself. In the first table below, you can see year-over-year unit sales for consoles in the US, followed by global sales in the table below that. Prior to June and July, when the surge occurred when it came to the Nintendo Switch 2, we had been seeing double digit declines in volume. This seems to be the result of broader economic concerns, as well as the continued aging of the consoles that existed at the time.

While this suggests that continued weakness is probable, if we strip out the Switch 2 sales, the good news is that the company is in a good financial position if we focus on its balance sheet. One of my complaints from prior articles was that the net cash position of the business had been declining. But from the first quarter of 2024 to the second quarter of 2024, net cash shot up from $1.06 billion to $4.18 billion. Today, it's even higher at $4.53 billion. For a business with a market capitalization of $10.55 billion as of this writing, that is a substantial amount of excess cash.

Management is also making other interesting moves. This has included the issuance of additional debt. In fact, it issued, earlier this year, $2.25 billion of 0% senior rate notes, plus it made available another $450 million of those, resulting in potential net proceeds of $2.68 billion. This debt, which is factored into my net cash calculation for the company, is convertible into shares at a price of $28.91 per unit. These don't mature until 2032. And if the stock does rise significantly, it could lead to investors experiencing dilution at an unfavorable price. But overall, I would say that this was a positive move that essentially helps to shore up the company's balance sheet.

It follows, I should mention, in April issuance of $1.50 billion, bringing in net proceeds of $1.48 billion for shareholders. Considering that this debt is also convertible, assuming the company does well, its overall net cash position could end up being quite a bit larger if management does not spend the cash in reckless ways. And this doesn't even factor in the prospect of the 59 million warrants that the company issued to current shareholders that entitle them to the right to purchase the stock at $32 up until October 30th of 2026, which would bring in gross proceeds of $1.9 billion if exercised in full. Needless to say, the company seems to be doing quite well from a balance sheet standpoint.

Because of the temporary surge in revenue we are seeing from hardware and accessories sales, it really is difficult to know what the true bottom line of the company will look like moving forward on a normalized basis. Having said that, in the chart below, you can see how much adjusted operating cash flow the company would need to generate each year to be fairly valued at a price-to-adjusted operating cash flow multiple of either 5, 10, or 15. You can see the same thing regarding EBITDA on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. Considering how well the company has done in the first half of this year alone, I would say that it stands a shot in the next year or two of hitting these targets. That's especially the case if the collectibles portion of the business continues to grow.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that I am impressed by how much GameStop has recovered. I honestly thought that the firm was likely to declare bankruptcy at some point in the distant future. But for now, things seem to be getting better. While risks remain and while this is far from being an attractive prospect, I do think things have stabilized enough to justify upgrading the company to a soft ‘hold’ at this time.

data.title: PPA: Accelerated Defense Spending Will Provide Further Momentum
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome MAK Invest as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (NYSEARCA:PPA) tracks the SPADE™ Defense Index, which gives exposure to companies involved in defense and aerospace operations. With quarterly rebalancing/reconstitution frequency, underlying companies within the index cover a wide spectrum of military and commercial aspects of the broader aerospace & defense (A&D) industry. The SPADE Defense Index comprises companies that generate a substantial chunk of their business through military and defense capabilities. It only incorporates companies that are considered systemically important to the U.S. defense sector.

Despite a strong rally in the stock since April, there is still room for PPA to make further gains amid encouraging developments related to the A&D sector. With the fund having significant holdings in some of the leading defense contractors, many of the underlying assets are well poised to benefit from rising defense spending across the globe. My analysis is backed by recent company-specific developments on securing lucrative defense contracts. I examined some of the top holdings—only in the context of their defense-related operations—to uncover favorable prospects that will allow for bullish momentum to continue.

Portfolio Concentration

PPA offers an adequate level of diversification with a total of 58 holdings and roughly 61% of the fund invested in large-cap value, growth, and blend equities. As the table below shows, the top 10 holdings account for around 56% of the portfolio. This results in a lower level of concentration risk compared with peers like iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA). Concentration risk of the portfolio is further reduced due to full replication of the SPADE Defense Index, which deploys a modified market capitalization weighting methodology. This methodology uses caps on the largest holdings to limit their influence on the overall index.

As shown above, PPA’s top 3 holdings make up around 24% of the total portfolio. For context, the top 3 holdings in iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF, which are the same 3 companies as PPA, account for 45% of the portfolio. Hence, PPA carries a significantly lower concentration risk. Such risk became apparent during the COVID-19 pandemic, when ITA’s higher exposure to commercial aviation giants like Boeing meant it fell more than PPA. Before that, it was leading.

In the context of global defense spending trends and geopolitical developments, I will examine PPA underlying assets. For this analysis, I ignore all risks and opportunities associated with commercial aerospace segments of the portfolio companies. The purpose is to understand the long-term implications of a surge in budgetary allocation towards defense for some of the biggest holdings within PPA.

Global Defense Allocations Touching New Highs

Global defense spending soared to around $2.5 trillion in 2024, as there were higher budgetary allocations across many countries. This accounted for 1.9% of global GDP in 2024 compared to 1.8% in 2023 and 1.6% in 2022. There was a particular focus on spending across European countries due to growing threat perceptions linked to Russia. Defense spending has remained a primary focus for many European nations, especially the NATO members, since Russia’s invasion of Crimea back in 2014. There was a unanimous agreement among the NATO members to spend 2% of their respective GDPs towards defense.

Given Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and their own sizeable military spending in recent years, President Donald Trump proposed allocation from NATO members to be increased to around 5% of GDP by 2035. But this idea has received mixed reactions from various governments, as many of them face budgetary constraints. Yet, 23 of the 27 EU members who are a part of the NATO alliance have committed to outlays amounting to at least 3.5% of their GDP. This will be backed by an additional 1.5% outlay on defense-related infrastructure.

The United States also has shown an aggressive intent despite initial claims of 8% defense budget cuts during the first quarter of 2025. President Trump recently sanctioned another $150 billion allocation towards the Pentagon’s defense spending. As per recent forecasts, 2026 spending by President Trump’s administration will surpass the $1 trillion mark, including costs related to the “Golden Dome” missile defense.

The U.S. government is expediting preliminary work on President Trump's "Golden Dome" missile defense shield that would rely on space-based sensors and interceptors. This multi-layered missile defense project will cost a whopping $175 billion in the coming 3 years prior to expected completion in early 2029. As per estimates provided by the Congressional Budget Office, post-initiation costs will amount to roughly $831 billion over the course of the following two decades. In recent developments, the Canadian government has also shown an inclination towards becoming a part of this missile shield initiative, as they take a stride in strengthening their defense.

Defense-Linked Prospects for Underlying Companies

GE Aerospace (GE) is the top holding in PPA with a weightage of 8.67%. Just a few weeks ago, the U.S. State Department approved an F-35 jets project for Poland worth $1.85 billion, for which GE Aerospace is the principal contractor. This is in continuation of the government’s focus on strengthening NATO and ensuring Europe plays its part by spending more. While this provides the bullish momentum, the sustainability is there thanks to the $5 billion indefinite-delivery/indefinite-quantity (IDIQ) contract from the U.S. Air Force, which offers military support to allied nations. These contracts will continue to power the company’s revenues in the future, which are also well supported by the backlog of orders. GE Aerospace has a $175 billion backlog, driven primarily by the expansion in the installed engine base. $20 billion worth of this backlog comes just from the defense portion, which adds stability to the defense revenues at a time when defense budgets are on the rise.

The Boeing Company (BA), carrying 7.58% weightage in PPA, continues to pave its way through loss of market confidence in recent history. Apart from an impressive demand backlog of $522 billion for commercial operations, the pipeline for the company’s defense & space segment also looks encouraging, with a backlog of $74 billion, as revealed in the Q2 2025 investor presentation. Boeing also recently finalized a contract worth $2.8 billion with the U.S. Space Force as part of the Strategic Satellite Communications program. This would involve the development of two satellite-enabled, space-based nuclear communication capabilities. Earlier this year they were awarded a very lucrative contract to build the U.S. Air Force's most sophisticated fighter jet, known as Next-Generation Air Dominance. The contract ensures a secure revenue stream worth hundreds of billions of dollars over the coming decades. The company has also been actively negotiating modifications to their existing contracts with the U.S. military forces, gearing up the total face values of those deals. Although margins remain in negative territory as of the first half of 2025, impressive revenue visibility and long durations of the contracts will ensure a successful operational turnaround for Boeing in periods to come.

RTX Corporation (RTX) carries the third-highest weightage of 7.54% in the ETF. After achieving a 17% growth in 2024 group revenues, the company posted $21.6 billion in revenues for the second quarter in 2025, a 9% jump compared to the same period last year. Net income for the quarter was $1.7 billion, resulting in a profit margin of 7.7%. Hence, the quarterly EPS of $1.56 outperformed the $1.45 analyst consensus. The company is pushing to consolidate its operations by shifting focus to core business lines. In that regard, RTX reached an agreement with TransDigm in June to sell Simmonds Precision Products for $765 million. Simmonds Precision Products falls under RTX’s Collins Aerospace division and manufactures fuel-sensing systems and related components.

As of Q2 2025, RTX has solidified its future revenue potential with a demand backlog of $236 billion, including $92 billion in business secured through defense contracts. The company has been bolstering its defense pipeline lately through new contracts and modifications. In late August, the Raytheon division was awarded a $1.7 billion modification to its contract with the U.S. Army regarding production support for lower-tier air and missile defense systems. Through this modification, the face value of the contract stands at a whopping $3.79 billion. Pratt & Whitney is another business division for RTX, which designs and manufactures aircraft engines and auxiliary power units. Prior to that, the division attained a contract modification worth $2.88 billion with the U.S. Navy for delivery of propulsion systems that are to be used for production of fighter aircraft. A CAGR of 16% in group revenues since 2019 enabled RTX to raise its quarterly dividend by 8% in June 2025, indicating promising prospects for the business.

Lockheed Martin (MT) is the fourth biggest stock in the portfolio, carrying 6.88% weightage. The company generates 97% of its revenues through long-term contracts with the U.S. government that ensure stable cash flows. It is one of the targets for President Donald Trump’s administration, which is currently pushing to buy stakes in several defense contractors. Following this news, LMT management also shared its commitment towards national defense in tandem with President Trump’s ambitions.

The company’s second-quarter results captivated investor attention, as revenues stood at $18.2 billion. However, operating profits were a bit lackluster at $748 million, a massive 65% drop on a year-on-year basis. Net margin for the quarter was close to 19%. These results dampened investor confidence in the short term, as the share price slid in the following weeks. Lockheed Martin is currently trading at a 23% discount to its all-time high in October 2024. However, it is important to look beyond the reported figures, as the profitability hit was due to one-off charges worth $1.6 billion related to underperformance of some legacy programs. The company still has an impressive demand backlog of $166.5 billion, more than 2x of 2024 annual sales.

General Dynamics (GD), at 4.69% weight, is another great addition. The company won a $1.85 billion modification to an existing contract in June, while more recently, the U.S. Navy exercised an option for adding DDG 51 destroyers to an existing multi-year contract. These clearly show the healthy spending in the sector, but GD also has a $103.7 billion backlog as of Q2 2025. This is a 14.5% growth in a matter of 6 months, showcasing the impact of Donald Trump’s presidency and increasing defense spending. The backlog stayed relatively stagnant during the last three years of the previous administration. This growth in backlogs adds to revenue visibility. If we consider the IDIQ contracts, the backlog goes up to $161.2 billion.

L3Harris Technologies (LHX) is similarly a beneficiary of increased spending not only from the U.S. government but also from NATO. It was selected by Poland for supplying the Viper Shield electronic warfare system, a country that is increasingly becoming a heavily armed state after the Russian invasion of Ukraine.

At this point I would like to point out an excerpt from L3Harris’ recent talk at the Jefferies Mining and Industrial Conference 2025. In response to a question about the Golden Dome, the chairman and CEO, Christopher Kubasik, responded:

Just the other day, I think the Senate brought forward an $878 billion budget for 2026. But the big news is the reconciliation bill of $155 billion. Normally, those would spread over 10 years, but the administration has been pretty clear that $155 billion will be spent in a 3- to 4-year period. So that's how you hear this $1 trillion number. So from the U.S. perspective, there's never been more money in the DoD budget than we have for '25 and definitely for '26.

The company reported $8 billion worth of new orders in Q2 2025. This has inflated the company’s backlog to $35.4 billion. With this healthy backlog, revenue growth, and margin expansion for the seventh quarter in a row, LHX’s revenues and profits are likely to stay healthy down the road, helping push the ETF up thanks to its 4.06% weightage.

With recent developments around how regional powers are trying to bolster their defense capabilities, the underlying holdings of PPA are expected to benefit from these trends. Going forward, many of the underlying companies could position themselves as key private sector stakeholders within the broader geopolitical landscape over many years to come. Strengthening of fundamentals will offer support to major underlying holdings, and investors should expect a healthy momentum to persist for PPA. Hence, PPA is expected to exhibit solid performance both in the short and long term.

Peer Comparison

PPA has two strong peers in the form of SPDR® S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR) and iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF. So far in 2025, these ETFs have outperformed PPA. However, it is worth taking a deeper look.

There are no red flags in PPA regarding liquidity, as the ETF has a 3-month average daily share volume that is over 5 times the median of all ETFs. It also falls in-between its peers ITA and XAR. A 15% turnover, compared to 42% for ITA and 36% for XAR, means the ETF doesn’t require much rebalancing, saving on unnecessary costs. Moreover, over the last 5 years, despite the higher expense ratio, PPA has outperformed its peers.

After a bullish rally between April and July, PPA has traded sideways. This has allowed it to close the gap with its 50-day moving average, as shown below.

The 50-day moving average is significant because it has acted as a strong support in the last two years. The RSI has also fallen below the overbought levels that were prevalent during the bull rally. This is a good opportunity for new investors to start building a position in the ETF. Since global disruptions in the form of geopolitical tensions and trade wars are becoming more common, it is also worth looking at how each of the ETFs responds to such events.

XAR recovered swiftly from the pandemic owing to its relatively equal distribution among mid-caps and large caps. ITA performed the worst due to its concentrated portfolio, especially in mega-cap stocks. During the April dip resulting from Trump’s tariff threats, XAR again proved its resilience and was the best of the three ETFs in terms of performance. PPA combines the best of both with its balanced exposure to the defense industry and mega caps.

Risks

A&D often becomes the go-to sector when geopolitical tensions arise. Investors should be aware that while these result in a strong demand for defense-related services, equipment, and technologies, they can also cause disturbances in the supply chain if conflicts linger. Similarly, such scenarios can affect the global economic outlook and consumer spending power, which can affect demand for commercial aerospace operations. As some of the constituents derive a considerable portion of their income from the commercial segment, such factors can hamper the performance of PPA, even though it is not part of my bullish thesis. At the moment, I do not consider these risk factors to be significant.

There are two other factors that I believe investors should be more concerned about. The first one is government spending, especially in light of the DOGE initiatives of the U.S. government. This risk is mitigated by the fact that while President Donald Trump has tried to reduce government spending, he has been quite vocal about spending more on defense. The second factor is that of tariffs and their impact on company finances, which cannot be ignored, though one could argue that investors have become used to the uncertainty surrounding it. I am not denying that this certainty isn’t weighing down on the ETF, but a positive sentiment resulting from clarity on tariffs is much more likely than negative sentiment due to its implementation, as that is already priced in in my view.

Recommendation: Buy

There is a widespread consensus that stocks within the aerospace & defense industry might witness a trend reversal in coming months, as President Trump is pushing to negotiate ceasefire deals in Ukraine and Gaza. However, these scenarios will not keep the U.S. government and European nations from ramping up their defense budgets. Those defense allocations are aimed towards long-term securities and defense capabilities of respective nations and would continue to be a focal point irrespective of developments in the near future.

PPA has been on a sharp rally since its lows during the first week of April. It has picked up from a price range of $105 at the time to a $151 range in the following 5 months. This might ask for a more cautious approach from investors to identify relevant entry points. But with solid fundamentals and promising prospects of major underlying assets, one can take confidence in having a bullish stance on PPA.

This makes me stay highly optimistic about the sector, and I would rate Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF a buy.

data.title: 2 Hidden Gems For A Blooming Retirement; Yields +6%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Often, the places and experiences that have the least amount of advertisement have the most authentic and enjoyable experiences. About an hour south of St. Louis, Missouri, lies a hidden gem, the Crystal City Underground.

This place has a rich history. It was a site for Silica mining since before the 1900s, and operations ceased in 1990. Once the pumps that kept water out were shut off, underground springs flooded the caverns, transforming the site into a serene subterranean world. This 150-acre underground lake forms a unique 2.25-mile kayaking tour.

On this tour, there’s a moment where you must lie flat to glide through the narrow “Squeeze Tube,” a passage where rock and water nearly touch.

This unique adventure, as beautiful as it is, remains largely inconspicuous – so much so that even many lifelong St. Louis residents have no idea it exists.

Investing is much the same. The best income opportunities are often tucked away, overlooked by Wall Street, and don’t get discussed on Jim Cramer’s show. Yet, they quietly operate for years and deliver steady and reliable cash to their shareholders. Today, we will look at two such investments.

Pick #1: KRP – Yield 10.5%*

Tariffs and trade deals continue to dominate the news. A commonality in all said deals with key nations includes pledges to buy more American energy, with Liquefied Natural Gas being the likely candidate for additional purchases. U.S. LNG exports reached a monthly record in August, with 9.33 million metric tons shipped. As of September 2025, the U.S. is the world's largest exporter of LNG. If these trade-related pledges turn into contractual agreements, they will be multi-decade long and will result in a massive increase in domestic production and shipment (Data Source: Bloomberg)

The E.U. is working to phase out its dependency on Russian energy, and LNG from the United States is emerging as a key replacement. The U.S. and Canada have abundant, low-cost natural gas resources, resulting in domestic prices being a fraction of those found in other major global markets. Source

U.S. Natural Gas has a bright future, and we will now examine an investment to draw royalties from the increased demand and production.

Kimbell Royalty Partners (KRP) owns and acquires mineral and royalty interests in oil and natural gas properties in the United States. KRP maintains direct mineral ownership in over 17 million gross acres with more than 131,000 wells. As a mineral royalty owner, the company has no associated operating costs or capital expenditures.

While the U.S. land rig count has dropped in recent quarters (7% sequentially) as a result of slowed drilling activity, KRP’s overall rig count dropped by only 2% to 88 rigs actively drilling on its acreage. Notably, KRP’s Permian and Haynesville rig count increased by 4 rigs and 5 rigs, respectively. Source

During the second quarter, KRP reported a run-rate average daily production of 25.4 Boe/d, almost flat sequentially. The company’s quarterly distribution was $0.38/share (from $0.42/share in Q2 2024). 100% of this distribution is considered Return of Capital, making it incredibly advantageous from a taxation standpoint.

Note: KRP is a C-Corp for tax purposes, and issues a 1099 to investors.

Most investors assume the YoY decline (or the sequential decline from $0.47/share distribution in Q1) is due to lower oil prices (oil was 33% of KRP’s production mix in the second quarter). While it may seem that way, it isn’t the full story.

During the second quarter, KRP reported total revenues of $86.5 million (vs. $76.5 million during Q2 2024). Net income, before any distributions, was $26.6 million (vs. $15.1 million in Q2 2024). Net cash from operating activities for 1H 2025 was slightly lower $126.4 million (vs. $131.9 million). During 1H 2025, KRP distributed $81.2 million to common unitholders, compared to $68.6 million for the first half of 2024. This is an 18.3% YoY increase, indicating KRP paid out more this year with oil in the $60 range vs. the $80 range last year. So, why did shareholders receive a smaller per share distribution?

KRP ended Q2 2025 with 93.3 million common units vs. 80.9 million common units outstanding as of June 30, 2024. This is a 15% YoY increase in unit count, resulting in shareholders receiving a smaller slice of the slightly larger pie. Now, if crude oil prices were comparable to last year, the distributions would have been higher as well, but that is always going to be variable and less predictable. The ideal situation would be higher production (Boe/d), higher commodity prices, so the DCF growth outpaces the equity dilution, but that isn’t under our control.

That may have been a complex way to explain the fact that KRP is a royalty firm that grows through acquisitions, primarily fueled by equity offerings, and pays variable distributions based on commodity prices and production volumes.

KRP affirmed its 2025 guidance on production levels, and its commitment to maintain a 75% payout on its distributable cash flow. The company intends to use the remaining 25% to pay down its line of credit. KRP remains well-positioned to be a leader in royalty asset acquisitions, to grow its average daily production rates, and deliver large payouts to investors.

Pick #2: CTA Preferreds – Up To 6.2% Yield

Corteva Inc. (CTVA) is a company focused on products for seeds and crop protection. The company was formed in 2019 after DowDuPont pursued a separation into three independent, publicly traded companies, with distinct focuses on agriculture (Corteva), materials science (DuPont), and specialty products (Dow).

During FY 2024, Corteva generated $16.9 billion in revenues and $3.3 billion in operating EBITDA. The company also returned ~$1.5 billion to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends. The chart below shows five annual dividend raises and a considerable 10% drop in the number of shares outstanding. Corteva expects to repurchase ~$1 billion of shares during this fiscal year.

During 1H 2025, Corteva reported operating EBITDA of $3.35 billion, up 14% YoY, and operating EPS of $3.33/share over this period, which places its current dividend at a comfortable 11% payout ratio. The common stock, however, yields under 1%, so we will look higher in the capital structure for a more livable yield at discounted prices.

  • $3.50 Series Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (CTA.PR.A)– Yield 6.2%
  • $4.50 Series Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (CTA.PR.B)– Yield 6.2%

As a result of the spin-off, two investment-grade BBB rated cumulative perpetual preferreds from the former parent company have come under Corteva, presenting a solid opportunity for those seeking steady and well-covered income. These carry annual dividend expenses that are measly for a large company like Corteva, and carry a very low coupon. No wonder they have been outstanding for the past 30+ years. Source

CTA-B has 1.6 million shares outstanding and is callable anytime at $120/share. It costs Corteva $7.5 million in annual dividend expenses. At current prices, CTA-B offers a 6.2% yield and ~65% upside to par. It will cost CTVA $200 million to redeem this preferred.

CTA-A has 700,000 shares outstanding and is callable anytime at $102/share. It costs Corteva $2.5 million in annual dividend expenses. At current prices, CTA-A offers a 6.2% yield and ~81% upside to par. It will cost CTVA $71.4 million to redeem this preferred.

It makes logical sense for Corteva to keep these preferreds outstanding, as a permanent, low-cost, foundational capital, for the foreseeable future. The company ended the second quarter with $2 billion in cash and cash equivalents. These preferreds cost the company ~$10 million annually, a fraction of the $1.9 billion net income generated by the company during 1H 2025. Corteva spends +$400 million on annual common stock dividends, making the cumulative preferreds an adequately covered security for consistent income.

Recently, there was a news item that Corteva is exploring a breakup to separate its seeds and crop protection businesses to unlock shareholder value. Both segments will be leaders in their respective domains, and are strongly profitable to support shareholder obligations. At this time, there is no confirmation that the company will be pursuing this split, nor is there any detail on which entity would take custodianship of the preferred securities.

Corteva preferreds carry low coupons, and come with a high cost of redemption. Backed by the steady financials of an industry leader, we expect both preferreds to remain outstanding for the foreseeable future, presenting a solid opportunity for perpetual qualified dividend income generation.

Conclusion

Some of the most authentic experiences are found far from the spotlight. This is largely true in the financial markets as well. Sometimes, the quietest investments are the most rewarding, and it’s our job to help you find them.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we keep our strategy simple; income is everywhere – if you know where to look. From hydrocarbon royalties to preferred shares and beyond, we seek out these hidden streams that get overlooked in the quest for the next big thing. Our portfolio is a deeply diversified portfolio, allowing us to draw strong income from the full breadth of the market.

The result is stability, diversity, and the steady flow of dividends that can fund a retirement lifestyle you desire and deserve. This is the beauty of our Income Method, and the power of income investing.

data.title: Valero Energy: Expecting Strong Results With Improving Crack Spreads And Renewable Diesel Market
data.description:
Thesis

Valero Energy (NYSE:VLO) is positioned for a strong quarter thanks to improving crack spreads and increased utilization from its refinery assets. This positions the company to continue to buy back stock through robust free cash flow generation.

Due to the cyclical nature of the refining industry, the company’s valuation remains misleading. Thanks to a strong macro environment, the company’s forward looking valuations are dirt cheap. This will become particularly evident as extremely weak quarters in Q4 and Q1 roll out of the trailing valuation.

Developments in California are also positioning VLO to become more profitable on the West Coast. The company has a been put into a win-win situation where the company can close down its most costly refinery (Benicia) or broker a deal with the state to receive subsidies for its operating costs. Either way, VLO’s margins are expected to increase as the refining market enjoys a balanced supply and demand dynamic over the coming years.

Refining Macro

The second quarter was a nice uplift for VLO from the start of the year. The company generated $1.3 billion in adjusted operating cash flow, up from $952 million in the prior quarter. This was driven by two main factors, improved crack spreads and better fleet-wide refinery utilization.

Starting with utilization, the company produced better-than-expected results, with total production yielding 92% capacity, up from 88% in Q1. Right off the bat, this helped grow earnings rather significantly. The company is projecting a comparable utilization rate in Q3, so these gains should be rather repeatable. I discussed VLO's sensitivity to utilization rate in my July coverage of the company for those who would get deeper into this topic.

Moving on to crack spreads, we also see very strong performance. Margins have steadily improved throughout the year. This can be directly traced to a shift in supply dynamics created by the closure of three refineries so far this year.

The closures in Houston and in Europe have provided for a strong export market. In particular, distillate spreads have been strong due to limited access to heavy crude barrels following Venezuelan sanctions and struggles at the Dangote refinery in Nigeria.

Notably, we can see the Gulf Coast is averaging up over 22%. This region amounts to roughly 2/3 of the VLO portfolio, so the performance here sets a strong tone for the direction of the quarter. With all of the other regions also performing strongly, Q3 looks set up to produce very strong results.

Changing Winds On The West Coast

One of the more interesting things happening in the refining industry lately is what has been going on in California. The announcements of the closure of the Phillips 66 (PSX) LA refinery and VLO's Benicia refinery near San Francisco appear to have caught the state's attention.

The state legislature first attempted to broker a sale of the Benicia refinery earlier this year. However, now that PSX has officially commenced the decommissioning process of the LA refinery, the state has ramped up its efforts.

Reports have circulated that the state is willing to pay VLO between $80 million and $200 million annually to keep the refinery in business. This equates to the state of California footing the bill in the range of $1-$4/barrel of operating expenses, making margins at Benicia significantly more inviting.

While it's still too early to tell if this effort will be successful, it creates an interesting win-win for VLO. If the company finds that this subsidy is sufficient to keep Benicia's doors open, it will have an asset with a lower capital footprint and a more constructive market now that the LA refinery has closed.

If the two sides still can't come to terms, the company will continue its current course. This helps VLO's shareholders in two ways. First, the accelerated depreciation of the asset will stop, providing an immediate uplift of $0.25/share in EPS every quarter.

Second, the company's net margin is likely to increase through addition by subtraction. Eliminating the operating loss created by this facility allows investors to realize the full benefit of its strong Gulf Coast assets. I also expect free cash flow available to investors to grow thanks to reduced sustaining capital requirements.

At this point, it's anyone's guess as to how the situation will unfold, but either way, the outcome looks positive for VLO shareholders.

Renewables Are Struggling But The EPA Is Likely To Give A Boost

The renewable segment has been challenging in 2025. Renewable diesel margins have continued to erode throughout the year as several facilities completed their ramp up process. This segment resulted in an operating loss in Q2, and margins have only deteriorated since.

However, upcoming provisions by the EPA are likely to provide some upside for this segment. The latest EPA proposal for refiner blending quotas is set to increase in 2026. Total blended volume requirements are expected to increase by 1.7 billion gallons next year, with an additional 400 million gallons slated for 2027.

Another potential driver for the sector is the resolution of the small refinery exemption dispute. The White House is currently evaluating a proposal from the EPA that would require large refiners to compensate for approximately 50% of the exemptions granted to small refiners over the last decade. While it appears that the White House is planning to strike a middle ground, the proposal could result in refiners such as VLO being required to produce at least 550 million gallons to achieve compliance with this new ruling.

The new production requirements bring with them an increase in associated RINs, which are the primary source of margin when producing a renewable barrel. The current market has more production capacity than available RINs, suppressing the crack spread. 2026 is positioned to increase total production requirements (and total RINs) by as much as 10%, positioning this segment for a strong rebound next year.

While this segment is dwarfed by VLO’s conventional refining assets, a turnaround in this segment would provide a sizable EPS boost. By even getting back to break-even cash flows, the renewable diesel segment would provide a $0.25/share EPS uplift from the second quarter's performance.

A Misleading Valuation

When I last covered VLO in June, the company traded at 11.5x EBITDA. Now with the stock up roughly 20%, the valuation has only elevated slightly. This valuation was significantly aided by a strong second quarter, with an EBITDA gain of nearly $1 billion over Q1.

In the chart below we can see how the weak performance in Q4 and Q1 is creating a significant drag on the company’s current valuation. As noted above, I expect Q3 to be even stronger than Q2. Following the Q3 earnings report, I expect VLO’s valuation to trade at least at 9x EBITDA, which is generally in line with analysts' expectations.

Risks

If 2024 and 2025 have provided any sort of lesson to investors, it’s that the cycle is volatile. Crack spreads can go from robust, to lean, and back to robust faster than you can cash those quarterly dividend checks. Right now things are pointing in the right direction from a supply perspective. However, demand can also be a significant factor.

Thus far, the economy has remained resilient despite record inflation coupled with interest rates at multi-decade highs. However, the recent round of job reports has painted a picture that perhaps initial cracks in the economy are starting to show. Any associated demand destruction would negatively impact crack spreads and VLO’s earning potential.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. The refining macro has continued to demonstrate strength in the third quarter. Crack spreads have continued to rise over Q2 levels which was very profitable.

2. VLO is set up for a win-win situation with its Benicia refinery. It is unclear how the situation will unfold but either outcome is positive for VLO’s earnings.

3. Revision to EPA requirements hold the potential to improve renewable earnings. Despite the smaller scale, getting the renewable diesel segment back to positive territory will have a meaningful impact to the stock.

4. The company trades at a roughly 12x EBITDA. This does not scream as a buy, but I believe this number is misleading. I expect significant improvement in the company’s valuation in the near term as weak quarters fall off the trailing twelve month metric and are replaced by stronger performances.

5. I continue to rate VLO as a BUY because it continues to be an industry leader with a fair valuation and multiple drivers for improvement.

data.title: Pagaya Technologies: Profitable Growth At A Bargain Price (Strong Buy)
data.description:
I am bullish on Pagaya Technologies (NASDAQ:PGY). The company’s AI-driven lending platform is now delivering profitable growth, and I believe the market is undervaluing its fundamentals. In August, Pagaya posted its second consecutive GAAP-profitable quarter, with Q2 2025 revenue up ~30% y/y and net income of $17 million.

Management raised full-year guidance on the back of this strength. In this article, I explain why I assign a Strong Buy to PGY.

Q2 Results Show Accelerating Momentum

Pagaya’s latest results underscore its rapid growth and improving profitability. Network volume (the total loans facilitated on Pagaya’s platform) hit $2.6 billion in Q2 2025, up ~14% y/y. This drove total revenue to a record $326 million for the quarter, a 30% y/y increase that beat analyst expectations.

Crucially, Fee Revenue Less Production Costs (FRLPC) reached $126 million, representing a 4.8% FRLPC margin on network volume. FRLPC essentially measures Pagaya’s gross profit on loans, and a 4.8% take-rate is up from ~3.8–4.2% a year earlier. In other words, Pagaya is not just growing in size, but earning more profit per dollar of loans facilitated, thanks to better economics with its funding partners.

Operating leverage is kicking in. Adjusted EBITDA jumped to $86 million in Q2 (a 26% margin), up 72% y/y. Expenses are growing much slower than revenues; for instance, Q2 general and administrative costs actually fell 37% y/y as Pagaya optimized costs.

GAAP net income was $16.7 million in Q2 2025, versus a $74.8 million loss in Q2 2024. This confirms that Pagaya has turned the profitability corner. In fact, first-half 2025 net income was $24.5 million, whereas the company lost $96 million in the first half of 2024. Achieving GAAP profitability is a key milestone that, in my view, validates Pagaya’s AI-driven underwriting model and unit economics.

To put the growth in perspective, here is a comparison of Pagaya’s Q2 2025 performance against Q2 2024:
