All key metrics are trending in the right direction. Notably, Pagaya’s take rate (FRLPC as % of volume) expanded to 4.8% from ~4.2% a year ago as the company shifted toward higher-margin products and funding agreements. This was achieved even as Pagaya deliberately pulled back in lower-margin segments (like single-family rental loans) to focus on profitable growth. The result is fewer low-margin loans, higher gross margins, and still solid network volume growth (network volume would have grown ~26% y/y excluding the trimmed segments). In short, Pagaya’s growth is high-quality driven by improved economics, not just volume for volume’s sake.

Growth Outlook and Guidance

On the Q2 earnings call, management struck an upbeat tone about Pagaya’s trajectory. The CEO noted it was Pagaya’s second straight quarter of record revenue and profit, citing “the sustainability of our growth” and “increasing demand for our product”.

As a result, Pagaya raised its 2025 outlook: full-year revenue is now expected at $1.25–$1.325 billion (up from prior $1.175–$1.3B guidance) and GAAP net income at $55–$75 million. The company also guided for Q3 revenue of $330–$350 million and net income of $10–$20 million.

These targets look attainable given Pagaya’s momentum. Krubiner highlighted that 30% of new loan originations now come from point-of-sale and auto loans, compared to only 9% a year ago.

The expansion into auto lending and point-of-sale financing is accelerating growth. Pagaya’s partnership network keeps growing. They now have +30 lending partners, including names like Klarna (KLAR), SoFi (SOFI), and Ally (ALLY), which feeds more data into Pagaya’s AI models and drives volume.

The total network volume over the last year reached $10.4 billion as of Q2 2025, up from $8.0B a year earlier. With strong secular tailwinds (banks and fintechs seeking AI-driven underwriting solutions), management confidently forecasts ~20% revenue growth for 2025 and sees a path to $25B annual network volume in 3-5 years. I share this confidence as Pagaya is riding a megatrend (AI in lending) and executing well.

Importantly, Pagaya is also expanding its funding capacity and product offerings. The company recently secured roughly $5 billion in new forward flow funding commitments from institutional investors like Castlelake. This enlarges Pagaya’s ability to finance loans off its balance sheet.

Additionally, new initiatives such as a direct marketing engine, “FastPass” instant approval, and affiliate partnerships are rolling out to boost partner loan volumes.

The president, Sanjiv Das, explained that Pagaya is helping partners pre-screen and target their customers for personal loans using Pagaya’s models. Essentially, driving more business to the network. These efforts should smooth out growth and deepen Pagaya’s integration with partners. Overall, I see multiple growth levers (new verticals, more partners, marketing tech, etc.) supporting Pagaya’s forward momentum.

Quant Ratings: What the Factors Say

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Ratings back up my bullish view on PGY. The quant system gives Pagaya a very high overall score, reflecting strength across key factors:

Momentum

PGY has been a top performer. The stock is up 246% in the past year, dramatically outperforming the market.

This price action earns Pagaya an A+ Momentum grade in SA’s system.

Even after this rally, I believe the momentum is fundamentals-driven. By contrast, peer Upstart (UPST) saw its stock spike earlier on AI hype but remains volatile amid inconsistent results.

Pagaya’s steady upward trajectory and improving financials give me confidence that the momentum can be sustained rather than “meme-like”.

Growth

It’s no surprise Pagaya scores an A+ grade for growth. Revenue has grown ~25% y/y and is guided to +20% for 2025. More impressively, FRLPC (gross profit) grew 26% in Q1 2025 and ~30% in Q2, outpacing volume growth, indicating expanding margins.

Pagaya’s EPS growth is striking now that it’s profitable: from a $(5.66) GAAP EPS in 2024 to an expected positive EPS this year.

Few fintechs are growing revenues 25–30% while flipping to profit. The growth factor reflects metrics like this robust top-line expansion.

Value

Despite the run to ~$44, PGY still screens as reasonably valued versus the sector, though the gap is narrower now. The quant panel shows an overall Valuation grade of C with a mixed but favourable tilt on forward operating multiples

On forward-looking metrics, Pagaya still trades at a discount to its sector peers. Its forward EV/sales of 3.0x sits about 11% below the sector median, while forward EV/EBITDA of 11.2x is 27% below, and forward EV/EBIT of 16.7x is nearly 19% below.

The offsets are its rich book and cash flow multiples. P/B of 8.7x TTM and 6.5x forward reflect a balance-sheet-light model that inflates book ratios, and P/CF of 24.5× runs above the median. Taken together, the data show Pagaya’s stock is still priced below the market on most operating multiples despite its run, which leaves room for multiple expansion if the company keeps executing.

Profitability

Pagaya’s Profitability grade sits at C-, reflecting a mixed but improving picture. On the plus side, the company already posts EBIT margin and EBITDA margin better than the sector. Its levered free-cash-flow margin and asset turnover ratio also exceed peers.

The negatives are legacy metrics still dragging the overall grade down. Net income margin and both ROE and ROA remain deeply negative on a TTM basis due to heavy investments and one-time charges, including 2024’s loan-vintage impairments. Gross profit margin also sits about 14% below the sector median.

EPS Revisions

Pagaya now scores a B for the Revisions factor, reflecting a very clear tilt toward positive estimate changes.

In the past three months alone, analysts have issued 8 EPS upward revisions versus just 1 downward and 10 revenue upward revisions with zero cuts.

Consensus EPS for December 2025 stands at $2.79, up 236% y/y, while 2026 and 2027 estimates are also rising (to $3.28 and $3.72, respectively) with double-digit y/y growth and strong one- and three-month positive trends.

The chart of consensus revisions shows a decisive turn higher through mid-2025 as Pagaya’s Q2 beat and raised guidance filtered through.

This factor captures improving Street sentiment and explains why the Quant system still assigns PGY a bullish “B” for Revisions even after the stock’s run: analysts continue to mark their numbers up in tandem with management’s outlook.

Comparing to Peers (Upstart, SoFi)

PGY combines the best of both worlds compared to peers: the AI/tech appeal and growth of an Upstart, but with a demonstrably better credit performance and partner-driven model that has now yielded real profits. Versus a broader fintech like SoFi, Pagaya is more focused and has superior margins. The market is beginning to wake up to Pagaya’s strengths, but I believe there is further to go, which underpins my strong bullish stance.

It’s helpful to contrast Pagaya with some peers to underscore why I favor PGY:

Upstart Holdings

Like Pagaya, Upstart uses AI models for consumer lending. But Upstart’s model has been far more volatile. Upstart primarily partners with banks to underwrite personal loans, and in 2022–2023 it suffered major setbacks as loan default rates spiked and funding dried up.

Upstart has yet to achieve consistent profitability and had to curtail lending volume when capital markets tightened. Even so, its shares still command a far richer multiple, about 7x forward EV-to-Revenue versus Pagaya’s 3x.

I view Pagaya as a better business with a safer model. Pagaya doesn’t hold loans on its balance sheet (beyond small risk retention pieces) and uses a two-sided network of loan originators and institutional investors.

This capital-light approach proved resilient: even when credit markets were stressed, Pagaya continued growing (network volume +17% in 2024), whereas Upstart’s volumes plunged.

Pagaya also proactively improved its underwriting. The company took impairments on its 2021–2022 loan vintages (clearing out the bad loans) and saw a rating upgrade from KBRA on newer loan pools, validating its AI model improvements.

Now with GAAP profits coming in, Pagaya is fundamentally ahead of Upstart, yet as noted, still valued lower by the market.

SoFi Technologies

SoFi is a fintech one-stop shop (loans, banking, investing). It’s not a direct competitor to Pagaya, but it provides context in fintech valuations. SoFi has grown rapidly but has struggled to attain profitability until late 2023.

It trades at 9.8x forward sales.

SoFi’s growth (30-40%) is on par with Pagaya’s, but its business model carries more balance sheet risk (SoFi holds loans and offers bank-like services). In contrast, Pagaya’s B2B platform model is capital-light and arguably more scalable (no need to fund loans with its own capital).

Both companies benefit from fintech adoption trends, but Pagaya’s niche (AI underwriting network) gives it a high-margin, B2B recurring revenue profile (via fees) that I actually prefer.

I mention SoFi mainly to highlight that Pagaya’s valuation is lower despite similar growth. If PGY were valued at SoFi’s multiples, the stock would be much higher.

Key Risks and Rebuttals

No investment is without risk, and Pagaya does face several notable risks that bears highlight, such as in this article by Apollonian Research. Here I address the key bearish arguments:

Balance Sheet & Funding Exposure

Skeptics argue that Pagaya could be forced to hold loans if investor demand falters, exposing it to credit risk. It’s true that Pagaya relies on institutional buyers (asset-backed securities investors, etc.) to fund the loans its AI approves.

In a severe credit crunch, if those funding channels dried up, Pagaya might have to retain more loans or drastically slow originations. We saw a glimpse of this risk in 2022 when ABS markets tightened. However, Pagaya has since taken steps to mitigate this.

The company now has ~$5B in forward flow agreements (basically pre-committed funding from large investors), providing more reliable capital. It also raised a $300M corporate debt facility in Q2. This bond gives Pagaya a war chest and flexibility to fund loans temporarily if needed in a nondilutive way.

Moreover, Pagaya’s improved loan performance (lower defaults due to better AI models) makes its loan pools more attractive to investors. In fact, Pagaya was able to issue multiple ABS deals in 2023–2024 and even achieved AAA ratings on an auto loan and a POS loan securitization. So while funding dependence is a risk, I believe Pagaya’s funding sources are robust and diversifying. The risk of a sudden liquidity crunch is much lower now than a year or two ago.

Macro & Credit Cycle Risk

Pagaya’s fortunes are tied to consumer credit health. A macro downturn or spike in unemployment could increase loan defaults and reduce lending activity across the board.

Bears note that Pagaya’s AI hasn’t been truly tested through a severe recession yet. This is valid; if a recession hits, originations might slow, and Pagaya’s take rates or fees could compress. However, I’d counter that Pagaya has built cushions.

The company’s fee model means it doesn’t get hit by credit losses directly (its partners and ABS investors do), so Pagaya’s revenue is more volume-based with some lagging impact if lenders tighten standards.

Also, Pagaya’s models continuously learn and have apparently become more conservative post-2021. The proof is in the pudding: recent loan cohorts are performing better, and credit ratings agencies upgraded Pagaya-linked loan securitizations, citing improved underwriting.

Pagaya’s FRLPC margin actually expanded during a high-rate environment. While a recession would hurt volume, I expect Pagaya would remain profitable even in a mild downturn, given its current 26% EBITDA margins.

The company is also expanding into counter-cyclical areas like auto refinancing and POS loans for essential goods, which could hold up better if the economy softens. Macro risk is real, but I view Pagaya as structurally more resilient than a traditional lender.

Underwriting Model Skepticism

Some bears question whether Pagaya’s AI underwriting truly adds value, or if the recent profitability is just due to cost-cutting and one-time fixes. They point out that Pagaya had to write off a chunk of its 2021–22 loan portfolio (the “toxic vintages”) and that achieving profitability after purging those losses doesn’t guarantee future success.

They also note that Pagaya’s take rate improvements partly came from exiting lower-margin loans (like single-family rentals) rather than pure AI magic.

These are fair observations.

My rebuttal is that Pagaya did what prudent management should do: they recognized the early model had issues, they cleaned up the mess (took impairment charges of ~$54M in Q2 2024 for credit losses), and they refined their algorithms.

Now the data shows improvement: FRLPC margin is up ~100 bps, and credit performance of 2023–2024 originations is stronger (as evidenced by rating upgrades and partner confidence).

One can’t "prove" the AI is superior except by results, and so far, results are trending positively. Pagaya’s partners keep expanding business with them, which suggests they see value (Pagaya helps approve more borrowers who perform well).

Also, focusing on higher-margin areas is smart business. Exiting a low-profit segment like SFR housing loans is a positive, not a negative, in my view. Yes, Pagaya must continue to prove its model each quarter. But with each profitable quarter and each successful loan vintage, the skepticism should abate. I see the recent profitability not as a one-off, but as the beginning of Pagaya’s validation phase.

Dilution and Share Structure

Another concern is Pagaya’s history of shareholder dilution. The company went public via SPAC, did a 1-for-12 reverse stock split in 2024, and issued equity (and convertible notes) to raise capital.

This resulted in a complicated share structure (multiple classes) and an increase in share count, which hurt early investors. The float is relatively small (~65 million Class A shares), with insiders and earlier Class B shares making up a chunk of ownership. Any further capital raises could dilute investors.

I acknowledge this risk, but I believe the worst of the dilution is over. Pagaya is now generating positive cash flow, reducing the need for fresh equity. The recent $300M bond raise was specifically done to avoid issuing stock and to bolster the balance sheet.

I expect any further financing for growth will lean on debt or internal cash generation, not dilutive equity. So while the share count jumped in the past (from SPAC merger, etc.), going forward shareholders should benefit from operating leverage without significant dilution. This risk is diminishing.

Conclusion

Pagaya Technologies is firing on all cylinders: revenue is surging, margins are expanding, and the company is now consistently profitable. Yet the stock’s valuation has lagged its fundamental performance, leaving considerable upside in my opinion.

We have an AI-driven fintech growing +25% with improving unit economics, trading at only ~2–3x forward sales and around 10x EBITDA, a clear disconnect.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant system rightly flags Pagaya as a Strong Buy, given its top-tier growth and momentum and still-reasonable valuation metrics. I see Pagaya as an asymmetric opportunity: the downside appears limited (the company has $205M cash on hand and is profitable, so bankruptcy or distress is off the table), whereas the upside from continued execution could be multiples of the current price.

I maintain a Strong Buy rating on PGY. In my view, the market is gradually re-rating Pagaya higher, but hasn’t fully priced in the long-term potential. The company’s own five-year vision (to triple network volumes to $25B+ and potentially generate 20-30% of that in free cash flow) suggests enormous earnings power ahead. Execution is key, but so far, Pagaya is delivering on its promises.

data.title: Constellation Brands: Sell This Stock, As Americans Are Drinking Less
data.description:
The alcohol industry is at a pivotal juncture. Shifting consumer preferences, economic pressures, and evolving regulatory landscapes are challenging legacy players while also opening the door to innovative possibilities. At the center of this transformation stands Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ), producer of beer, wine, and spirits, such as Corona Extra, Modelo Especial, Kim Crawford, and Robert Mondavi Wines.

STZ doubled from its lows in 2020 at around $132 a share, to over $271 a share in July of 2023. It has been on a steep decline, specifically since the start of this year, and now trades back where it did a decade ago at $135 per share.

I rate STZ a sell. The stock has a fair valuation and is working toward new alcoholic and non-alcoholic ideas, but there are way too many headwinds that seem likely to stay in the near term. Guidance has been lowered, beer sales are dropping, financials plummeted in the last twelve months, and peers have outperformed.

Updated Guidance

A few weeks ago, management provided an early guidance update before Q2 2026 numbers are released in October. Unfortunately for STZ, the update was mostly negative and reveals the gloomy outlook they see for the foreseeable future.

Net sales were expected to be 1% up to 2% down and have shifted to an anticipated 4% to 6% reduction. Beer sales projection moved further in the red, while guidance for wine and spirits remains the same, but it is expected to be down 17-20%.

Operating income for beer specifically has taken an even more dramatic shift and has moved from expectations of 1% down, to about 8% down with the update.

Lastly, adjusted EPS guidance was $12.60-$12.90 prior, and has been changed to $11.30-$11.60 after the update. Fiscal year 2025 adjusted EPS was an all-time high of $13.77. The stock tumbled during this period and now is seeing earnings likely to be about 17% lower than the year prior.

By the Numbers

Cash from operations has been a positive point of business for STZ, and has continued to increase, reporting FY25 of $3.15 billion. Free cash flow has also been robust, matching their highs from 2021 at about $1.94 billion for FY25. This allows for them to provide their strong dividend, which yielded 3.74% TTM.

On the other hand, their balance sheet is struggling. Total assets have drifted consistently lower since about 2021, whereas total liabilities have remained about the same over that period. Current assets are $3.72 billion, outweighed by current liabilities at $4.04 billion, resulting in a current ratio of 0.92x. It’s the first time their current ratio has slipped below 1.0 in many years, as liquidity worsens for the firm. Cash per share for FY25 was 0.38, also the worst in a long time, and net debt is currently $11.5 billion, right in the middle of the recent average by year.

Beer in Trouble

Drinking habits in the U.S. are changing. A recent Gallup poll resulted in 54% of people saying they drink alcoholic beverages, the lowest rate in nearly 90 years. Barclays reports that they expect beer volumes to drop 5% in 2025, and 2% annually thereafter.

What’s worse for STZ is their dependence on Hispanic beer buyers, which is where they get about 40% of their beer revenue. STZ has a high-end Mexican beer portfolio that has done them well in the past but is now seeing significant headwinds. Hispanic consumers are seeing immigration policy lead to fewer social gatherings. STZ reported in their recent update that over 80% of Hispanics are concerned about the socioeconomic environment, causing a decline in discretionary spending. Health habits and immigration policy are changing in America; both are detrimental to business for STZ right now.

Peers & Valuation

STZ has underperformed its beer, wine, and distillery peers this year. On the profitability side, they sit okay, with the best EBITDA margin at 37.74%. However, on the net income side, they are the only company in the group with a recent negative net income, which is -$442 million in the last twelve months, and -4.40% from a margin perspective.

STZ has a forward PE ratio of 12.25, which sits in the middle of these four. Molson Coors (TAP) is down this year, but much less than STZ, and is offered at a further discount. Anheuser-Busch (BUD) is up 17% this year and still has a lower price/sales and half the price/book than STZ. The sector median PE is 18, and while STZ is at a discount, it’s not by as much as it seems, and is lacking compared to better-performing competitors.

Risks to Thesis

There are a few risks to my thesis and opportunities for STZ to turn things around. Management noted that they gained market share in 49 out of 50 states in beer business volume in Q2 this year. I pointed out that peers look better than STZ from several angles, but they seem to be improving in beer sales across the country.

As the healthier trends continue, STZ seeks to provide consumers with refreshed alcohol options as well as non-alcoholic beverages. Corona Sunbrew was rated the number one new beer brand, and Corona Non-Alcoholic was the number two dollar share gainer in that segment. This industry will need to keep shifting gears to attract customers, and so far, compared to others, STZ has been doing fine with its innovation.

Conclusion

I think STZ is hanging in there with its refreshed beverage options, and strength in cash from operations. But the downfall has started, and I don’t think it's anywhere near its trough yet.

Beer and wine sales are plummeting across the country, as consumers take a more health-conscious approach. Because of this, STZ management felt it was necessary to provide an update even before earnings to let investors know how bad it could get. Hispanics are a large chunk of their business and have showcased an even larger drop in beer sales due to immigration policy. Peers have performed better than STZ, and that is also not including the prominence of local craft brewers becoming a bigger part of the beer industry.

There is too much standing in the way of STZ. Sell this stock.

data.title: DocuSign's Growth Inflection Is Here: A Dip-Buying Opportunity Ahead
data.description:
DOCU Remains Attractively Valued As IAM Delivers Improved Cross-Selling & Renewed Growth Opportunities

We previously covered DocuSign, Inc. (NASDAQ:DOCU) in April 2025, discussing how the stock was even more attractively valued after the selloff, thanks to the growth acceleration through IAM as observed in the robust sequential growth in its billings.

Combined with the growing multi-year RPOs, the rich profit margins, the higher Free Cash Flow generation, and the healthy balance sheet, we had believed that the SaaS company was likely to outperform prior levels, resulting in our buy rating then.

DOCU 1Y Stock Price

Since then, DOCU has found a floor at the $67s by early August 2025 before rallying by +25.8% to the $84s range by the time of writing, with a similar development also observed in some of its enterprise SaaS peers in varying degrees.

Much of their tailwinds are naturally attributed to the reiterated durability of enterprise spending trends in the Q2 '25 earnings season, as similarly reported by Microsoft (MSFT) and Adobe (ADBE), with it naturally driving the market's exuberance surrounding the sector to new heights.

This is significantly aided by the Fed cutting interest rates in the recent September 2025 FOMC meeting while signaling further rate cuts in 2025, which is likely to drive an accelerated macroeconomy normalization while relieving the pressure arising from the ongoing tariff uncertainties.

For now, DOCU has further fed the rally with a notable acceleration in its subscription revenue growth to $784.4M (+5.1% QoQ/ +9.3% YoY) and billings to $818M in FQ2'26 (+10.6% QoQ/+12.9% YoY).

This is compared to a year ago at +7.1% YoY and +1.8% YoY, respectively, with it underscoring the growing success of the new Intelligent Agreement Management [IAM] platform, as the AI-native end-to-end agreement platform drives improved consumer efficiency while expanding its total addressable market across different verticals.

These reasons are also why DOCU has been able to report the growing cross selling trends beyond the legacy eSignature business, the increased adoption of the IAM offerings, and the customers' higher consumption trends, with it already triggering the expanding dollar net retention rate of 102% (+1 points QoQ/ +3 YoY).

This is on top of the robust total customer count growth at 1.74M (+8.7% YoY) and large customers at over $300K in annual spending at 1,137 (+6.6% YoY), despite the tough comparison from a year ago at +11.1% YoY and +6.6% YoY, respectively.

At the same time, thanks to the aggressive sales incentives and the new self serve platform, DOCU has reported "healthier early renewals than expected" and an increased shift to annual billing contracts as well, allowing them to raise the FY2026 subscription revenue guidance to $3.12B (+7.5% YoY), up from the original guidance of $3.06B (+5.5% YoY) offered in the FQ4'25 earnings call.

These reasons may be why the consensus has raised their forward estimates, with DOCU expected to report accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +7.6%/ +8.5% through FY2028.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +5.9%/ +1.2% a year ago, albeit still decelerating from the historical 6Y growth profile at +27.3%/ +84.5%, respectively, with it exemplifying why the stock has had the painful meltdown by -86.9% between the July 2021 peaks and the October 2023 bottom.

Even so, we also can not deny the impact of DOCU's renewed growth opportunities from the IAM, as observed in the management's raised guidance and the consistently raised forward estimates, with it underscoring why the stock has been able to more than double from the October 2023 bottom levels.

Most importantly, the SaaS company has been able to report zero debts on its balance sheet, with the rich cash flows used to cancel out the SBC expenses, as observed in the relatively stable share count and balance sheet health over the past few quarters.

DOCU Valuations

As a result of the improving monetization cadence, we can understand why the market continues to hold DOCU stable at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 22.20x, near to the 1Y mean of 22.43x and the 3Y mean of 21.62x, while moderating from the prior 5Y mean of 77.89x.

When comparing to the sector median of 24.33x and its enterprise SaaS peers, including:

  • Adobe at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 17x/ the 5Y P/E mean of 32.19x,
  • Zoom (ZM) at 14.51x/39.04x, and
  • Microsoft at 32.80x/31.89x, respectively,

it is apparent that DOCU is still somewhat reasonably valued at current levels, despite the double-digit recovery over the past two years.

So, Is DOCU Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

DOCU 5Y Stock Price

For now, we have observed the emergence of DOCU's uptrend support lines since the October 2023 bottom, with it providing great insights for those looking to add the stock.

DOCU's Seemingly Overbought Levels

On the one hand, it is undeniable that DOCU appears to be overbought at current levels, with the elevated Relative Strength Index [RSI] and trading volumes implying a peaking in the near term.

On the other hand, historical trends already suggest a near-term reversal to the intermediate support levels of $77s anyway, before the stock's upcoming breakout beyond the prior resistance levels of $90s.

This reason is also why we believe that DOCU's upcoming correction presents a great dip buying opportunity for those still looking to add, especially since it may trigger an improved margin of safety to our fair value estimates of $79.40, based on the LTM adj EPS of $3.58 (+7.1% sequentially) and the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 22.20x.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $4.53, the upcoming pullback may trigger an expanded upside potential to our long-term price target of $100.50 as well.

As a result, we are reiterating our Buy rating for the DOCU stock, with the caveat that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer and add nearer to our Buy ranges of between $68s and $77s for a lower dollar cost average.

Given the potential -14.2% downside at the midpoint from the current levels, some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Cidara's Flu Drug: A Phase 2 Triumph, A Phase 3 Gauntlet
data.description:
From Antifungal to Flu Fighter: A Cidara Follow-Up

My last coverage of Cidara Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CDTX) was in May 2023. Cidara is advancing CD388, described in Nature as a “first-in-class antiviral drug–Fc conjugate,” to reduce flu viral replication and incidence. So, this is a unique approach. Flu vaccines (prophylaxis) rely on immune responses to work, so their efficacy is variable in vulnerable populations. On the flip side, post-exposure prophylaxis (after someone has already been exposed to the virus) includes antiviral drugs like TAMIFLU. So, Cidara is positioning itself in a unique niche in prophylaxis with a high unmet need.

Back to my article: I reviewed Phase 2a influenza trial results, which were promising (caveats, like small N, aside) and lauded the company’s transition from the challenging antifungal treatment market into immunology per its proprietary Cloudbreak platform. My recommendation was Buy, and CDTX has since skyrocketed 250% on the heels of Phase 2b flu data that was revealed earlier this Summer.

The following article reassesses Cidara in light of recent developments and concludes that while Phase 2b data is awesome, the road ahead is quite challenging.

NAVIGATE Trial Success Puts CD388 on the Map

On June 23, 2025, Cidara revealed topline Phase 2 data (NAVIGATE) on CD388, via subcutaneous injection, inspiring a 100%+ stock rally. The trial was a massive success by all measures, meeting all efficacy endpoints for all dose groups. Notably, there was a clear dose response, which also validates CD388’s effectiveness.

The data is highly statistically significant, and unlike most Phase 2 trials, this was a very large study (5,000 adults who are not at-risk of developing complications from the flu). Recall that the Phase 2a data I cited in my last analysis only enrolled ~60 healthy participants.

To compare with flu vaccinations, CD388’s top dose (76%) does sit above many historical vaccine VEs (efficacy), which can vary around 50% depending on the season. Moreover, the result is on par with mRNA vaccines. However, CD388 is not looking to dethrone vaccinations in healthy individuals. Rather, management is targeting at-risk populations (e.g., elderly, immunocompromised) who have mixed responses to standard-of-care. CD388 does have Fast Track status in high-risk populations.

The Road Ahead: Navigating the Complexities of Phase 3

So, CD388 data will have to be translated into high-risk populations in Phase 3 studies. This will, undoubtedly, require higher standards. For instance, I suspect that regulators/payers will want hard outcomes like hospitalizations and all-cause mortality. No one is going to pay for CD388 just because it prevents people from getting a fever! Moreover, current recommendations call for high-risk patients to be vaccinated. Ethics and guidelines will come into place in Phase 3 (we are testing high-risk, vulnerable people now) and will likely require an add-on (CD388 + vaccine versus vaccine alone) or non-inferiority/superiority data. Winning against a placebo in a Phase 2 trial is much easier work.

Something else to point out: although CD888 isn’t a vaccine and doesn’t rely on host immunity, high-risk physiology can differ from healthy populations (impacting PK/PD, tissue penetration, etc.). Think about what the Phase 3 population will look like as well. These cohorts will have higher AE baselines and are on lots of drugs. During med pass, when I have the privilege of caring for the sick and elderly in my part-time role as a nurse, it becomes very evident how many medicines people are on at baseline. It is actually perplexing and certainly has some kind of impact on the effectiveness/absorption/peak/etc. of other drugs. So, it may prove more challenging to maintain clean safety in this population. Moreover, should mRNA flu vaccines, like Moderna’s (MRNA) 1010, become standard, particularly in high-risk groups, it would raise the SOC bar.

Therefore, CD388’s true edge must be immune-agnostic protection in vaccine-hyporesponsive patients. To put it bluntly, guys, CD388 still has a long way to go before I find myself injecting it in hospitals near you.

At a Glance: CD388 vs. The Competition

Attribute CD388 (Cidara) Conventional Flu Vaccines mRNA Flu Vaccines
Modality Drug-Fc conjugate (neuraminidase-targeted antiviral) Inactivated/recombinant/live vaccines (antigen exposure) mRNA encoding HA antigens
Dependence on Host Immunity No (direct antiviral) Yes Yes
Typical Regimen Single SC/IM dose per season Single IM dose per season Single IM dose per season
Key Population Studied (to date) Healthy, unvaccinated adults (18–64), placebo-controlled (NAVIGATE) Broad general population (real-world VE) Adults ≥50, active-controlled vs licensed vaccine
Headline Efficacy Up to ~76% protection (dose-dependent) over ~6 months ~40–60% VE in matched seasons (variable) Superiority vs. standard-dose vaccine in ≥50 (rVE ~+27%)
Comparator in Trials Placebo Test-negative real-world benchmarks Active comparator (licensed vaccine)
Development Status P2b positive; Phase 3 planning (add-on to vaccination likely) Standard of care, approved P3 positive; filings planned by sponsors

Financial Health

Financially, the recent stock rally inspired management to raise ~$400 million in a June 2025 equity offering, giving the company $516.9 million in cash, equivalents, and restricted cash – sufficient to fund a large Phase 3 trial. Cidara carries minimal debt. After selling its antifungal drug REZZAYO, Cidara does not recognize revenue in its day-to-day operations. In fact, Janssen Pharmaceuticals (JNJ) terminated its CD388 agreement last year. In the past six months, the company has utilized $63 million for operations (a proxy for “cash burn”). Assuming no changes to expenses/revenues, this affords it a “cash runway” of roughly four years.

Applying the 'Dizzy Framework': What's Next for CDTX?

Looking ahead, Cidara intends to have an End-of-Phase-2 meeting with the FDA in preparation for Phase 3 design (e.g., size, duration, endpoints, etc.). Given that Cidara’s pipeline is in earlier stages, CD388 updates have a large impact on CDTX's valuation. However, I wouldn’t classify the FDA meeting, pegged for Q3, as a Tier-1 (decisive) event. Rather, it is supportive (Tier-2). Good news would look like a requirement of just one Phase 3 trial, which would reinforce a bullish thesis. Conversely, hurdles (the requirement of two Phase 3 trials) may permit position sizing adjustment, but cannot, by nature, be “thesis-breaking.”

Within this context, the next quarter or two are expected to be "low" on the Tilt Index.

A "low-to-moderate dizzy" period calls for normal position sizing and tighter trading bands. Adding on dips or trimming any irrational spikes may be wise as well. With a lack of Tier-1 events for some time, CDTX could simply trade in line with the broader market or biotech sector.

For now, CDTX trades near its 50-day MA. In the future, it could feasibly fall back to the 200-day MA on no news. This could be interpreted as a “buy-with-both-hands zone.” Meanwhile, we may consider adding under the 50-day MA, which has served as a springboard in the past.

Conclusion: Downgrading to Hold After a 250% Run

In conclusion, NAVIGATE data does de-risk CD388. Although other Cloudbreak assets are in earlier-stage development, NAVIGATE data also serves as a powerful proof-of-concept for the platform itself. With that said, I’d be careful not to extrapolate too much from NAVIGATE data. CD388’s path ahead appears more challenging in sick populations. Moreover, finding its niche in an evolving market (e.g., mRNA flu vaccines nearing authorization and real-world use) won’t be easy despite its novelty (Cidara does, after all, have solid clinical proof where others have failed). All things considered, I think the market fully appreciates Cidara, so I’m tempted to “step off the accelerator” and downgrade from Buy to Hold in light of the recent stock rally and the potential dearth of catalysts.

To clarify (people often misinterpret), Buy → Hold is not necessarily a negative judgment. It assumes one bought the position, is holding, but refraining from adding more at current prices. I’m curious to see how the stock settles months after NAVIGATE and what the FDA wants to see in a Phase 3 study before making further decisions. Moreover, CDTX is one for a barbell portfolio. This is still a high-risk/high-reward stock, folks

data.title: Investing Outlook As Central Banks Begin Rate Cuts
data.description:
Markets continue to move higher after both the U.S. Fed and the Bank of Canada cut their key interest rates earlier this week, but the case for future rate cuts remains murky. Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist with TD Wealth, joins MoneyTalk to break down what investors should be watching for when it comes to the continued market rally and the potential path for interest rates.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - North American markets are at record highs. Central banks are embarking on a new rate hike -- rate cutting cycle. Oh-ho-ho! Don't get those messed up. But as we head into the months that are generally weak for the markets, can this run continue? Joining us now is Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist with TD Wealth. Brad, great to have you back on the program.

Brad Simpson - Greg, it's great to be here.

Greg Bonnell - Our mission always when we have you on the show is to make sense of the world. So let's start with these equity markets hitting new highs. This traditionally isn't supposed to be a kind month for equities. What's going on?

Brad Simpson - Yeah, okay, well, it's right out of the gate. Kind of my goal here today is often I think that we live in a world that might be a post-fact society. So I think maybe why don't we, middle of a market that clearly has a little bit of froth right now, maybe we'll do a little myth busting right out of the gate and start kind of framing it that way. So, I actually brought a chart along that looks at volatility. Now, I think--for that one way up there, it's about rate cuts. Let me come back to that one. Let's go to volatility if it -- perfect.

I think we enter, we typically enter the fall and think, "oh no, we're going to have a big correction," or worry about that and higher volatility. So what we're looking at on the screen here is we went back about 30 years and looked at market corrections over that period of time. And so since 2000 -- and said, okay, as we move into October and November, what has really happened? Now, why we're worried about fall market corrections? Because we've had some big ones, right? We've had 2007 and 2008, what was that? Of course, we had October '87. But if you look at that 30-year cycle, actually what you see is that kind of plus or minus in between September and October, which are usually the big ones, on average, since that 20-year period, you're, on average, up or down 1.5% between those couple months.

The green is--what you're seeing is the VIX, which is volatility. And so I think a little bit, I think that the myth-busting is a little bit is, yes, we've had on the outliers, so if the world was a bell curve, you have on the outside, we have had extreme market downsides in the fall. But on average, most of the time, it's actually been pretty good. And if you go all the way back to 2020 -- or in 1929, so throw in the big, huge correction there, on average, your up or down cycle again is somewhere between up or down 1.5% between those two.

So yes, we get more volatility. But the green line and seeing the volatility up, I think, is what happens is that markets become much more quiet in the summer. The liquidity drives up a little bit in there. Peoples' activity drives up a little bit. This is a bit of a nudge. It's a bit of a push to kind of wake you up again, start you up again and to look and go -- wait a minute here, let's get back in focus a bit. And I think that's, if anything, that's what that little bump you see in the VIX is. But on average, let's be careful about making any big assumptions.

Greg Bonnell - Generalizations. The markets, we know, are forward-looking instruments. We have weakening job markets on both sides of the border. We have concerns about our economy, what could happen with the U.S. economy. What's the market telling us about recession fears?

Brad Simpson - Yeah, well, I guess, maybe the perfect place to start there is, as you said on the way in, we had an interest rate cut from the Bank of Canada, and then, of course, we had the big one, the interest rate cut coming from the Fed, and so 25 basis points. So again, and I'll use this as a little bit of a myth-busting tour, if you will.

And so, I brought this chart along just again to help frame this a little bit. We're going to see in the next couple of days from investment firms all over the place showing that, okay, we're at the beginning of an interest rate cycle. And what does this mean? What I'm showing here is that we're not at the beginning of an interest rate cutting cycle. I'm calling this the Ross and Rachel Rate Cut.

Greg Bonnell - We're on a break. It's not a break.

Brad Simpson - Right? Exactly. This rate cutting cycle started in September 2024. We had a 50 basis points cut in 2024, and we had another 25 in November, another 25 in December, and we've been on a break.

And so my starting point for folks who are kind of watching today is that every time you see this, "we're at the beginning of an interest rate cycle," ignore it. Because we're not. We're halfway through. Right now, the market expectation is that there's going to be four more rate cuts - two more this year, two more next year. Call that 100. The first phase was 100. The last phase is 100.

The important part of this chart is what I want to kind of hit home on what we need to think of as investors. And this is that '74 and '80, you can see it on the left-hand side, showing extreme examples of how this is really positive, right? The market really took off when the interest rates cut kicked in! That's because the recession caught everybody a little off guard. The market got hit. This is showing returns off the bottom. When you look at the ones where you have the green and it's up, it's actually where there wasn't a big market correction. You actually had either a shallow recession or no recession, and the market continued to go up, but it didn't have that big bang downwards, so the average number on the way back up is less. You get me on that?

So when you look over on the far left, what we're saying is if we're not at the beginning of an interest rate cutting cycle, what we're showing here is on the far left-hand side is September '24 is looking at what the market has done since that rate cut. It's a 17.5% return. Meaning that if your call here is that the market basically is saying there won't be a recession, and they're all in. And so the best-case scenario is that this is a very, very optimistic call on where we're going to go from here. That's where we're seeing some of the frothiness in this. And I think we have to, a little bit, is folks need to take a bit of a break here.

And why this is important is that this isn't the beginning of a new cycle. The beginning of the new cycle was back in September. And so we're well into that new cycle now. And now we have to think about, okay, we're halfway through this interest rate cycle here. How are we going to be making decisions from here? And I think that -- because your starting point on this is very different. If you're trying to say, "well, this is the beginning," no. And so it's at well halfway through.

So let's start making portfolio decisions based on we're a lot later in the game than this. The question is, is if you were looking at something to square or hang this on, is somewhere a period between 1994 and 1999. If you wanted to find something, well, let's try to find some similarities, too. We were getting interest rate cuts in 1994. We were getting those interest rate cuts because of a global emerging market bonds concern, right? It was called the "Tequila Sunrise," worrying about Mexico's bond market. And then, of course, into '98, we had what we called "the Asian Contagion." We had problems in the emerging market there, and that filtered through the rest of the economy.

Of course, meanwhile, we had all that, we had the build-out and the growth of the technology side. You take which way is this global economy going, cutting interest rates, you put a technology layer in there, and at that time it was the internet, and you start looking at some of the valuations here, the question people start to circle is, "Wait a minute," right?

Greg Bonnell - Is it the same? Is it different? What are we talking about here?

Brad Simpson - And I think maybe that's one of the things that we should probably work through today, too.

Greg Bonnell - Okay, we've got lots of time to work through stuff. I want to talk about, because we've been showing, we have central banks cutting. The market's not pricing in a recession. So what does it mean for the fixed-income markets? In this environment, what are we thinking about fixed income?

Brad Simpson - Well, I think maybe the starting point for the fixed income side is that the -- we have memories and nightmares of the last few years. Like, you know, let's face it, interest rates have backed up. You kind of look at where our starting point here is saying, "well, if central banks are cutting interest rates, clearly the yields are going to be coming down and I'm going to start seeing a big positive movement in my bond portfolios." Well, that's not what we've seen. We've seen short-end bonds going down, but of course, we've seen the longer end staying up.

So for us, I think maybe a two-point here is a lot of what I've said over the last few months is, and wrote about and how we've been allocating is, saying "Let's keep our duration short, let's think about how we are getting the interest rate return, and let's not worry and think too much about the capital gain part of this." I think we have moved along the spectrum here a little bit, and I think the first thing you want to do is you want to look at anything that is kind of junky in nature or high-yieldy in nature, triple-C kind of in nature, anything there. I would look at that as an equity investment right now, right?

I'd say the risk on it is closer to equity risk. And so if it's equity risk and that the spread between that and an investment-grade bond and a bond is actually quite low, I think you want to really be very, very cautious in that part of the market.

We think for investment grade, there's still an enormous amount of demand in the marketplace for investment-grade bonds. The yields are pretty good. Keep your yields shorter duration into one or two years in that zone. That's what positioning as an organization we're in, and we think that makes a lot of sense.

On the government side, we think that you can probably add a little duration. You can move out here a little bit. And kind of in that 5- to 10-year zone, I'm not saying sell the ranch and go out there, but just we've moved out our exposure a little bit, and we think that there's some opportunity on that side.
